1: egeneto de en tō ton ochlon epikesthai autō kai akouein ton logon tou theou — as the crowd was pressing upon him and hearing the word of God — epikeimai (to press upon, lie on) describes the physical pressure of an eager crowd. Luke identifies Jesus's teaching as ton logon tou theou (the word of God), not merely rabbinic instruction; this identification is programmatic in Luke-Acts where the apostolic message is consistently called ho logos tou theou (Acts 6:7; Luke 12:24; Luke 13:7). limné Gennésaret — the Lake of Gennesaret — Luke's preferred name for the Sea of Galilee, derived from the fertile plain on its northwest shore.
2: kai eiden ploion dyo hestōta para tēn limnēn — he saw two boats standing by the lake — the fishermen (halieis) had come in after a night's fruitless fishing and were washing their nets (eplynon ta diktya) — daytime net-cleaning after the night-fishing failure Luke has established in advance of the miraculous catch narrative. The detail of net-washing indicates the end of the working night, not the beginning; the fishermen are finished and available.
3: embas de eis hen tōn ploiōn, ho ēn Simōnos — stepping into one of the boats, the one belonging to Simon — Jesus requisitions Simon's boat as a teaching platform before the miraculous catch. The boat-as-pulpit (ekathisen ... edidasken = sitting, he was teaching) follows the seated-teacher pattern (cf. Luke 4:20); the lake amplifies the voice for the crowd on the shore. Luke establishes the relationship between Simon and Jesus (the boat-sharing) before the call narrative of Luke 5:10-11, making the call the natural consequence of a relationship already begun rather than an abrupt summons to a stranger.
4: hōs de epausato lalōn, eipen pros ton Simōna — when he stopped speaking, he said to Simon — the teaching ends and the testing begins. Epanagage eis to bathos kai chalasate ta diktya hymōn eis agran — put out into the deep and let down your nets for a catch — bathos (depth, deep water) is where fish cannot be seen from shore; the daylight instruction contradicts fishing practice (Galilean fishermen fished at night in shallow water with torchlight to attract fish). The command makes no pragmatic sense and is obeyed as an act of trust.
5: kai apokritheis Simōn eipen, Epistata, di holēs nyktos kopiasantes ouden elabomen — Simon answered: Master, working hard all night we caught nothing — Epistata (master, commander, overseer) is Luke's exclusive title for Jesus in this pre-discipleship scene; it is not rabbi (teacher) or kurios (Lord) but the word for a military or civil commander. The professional fisherman acknowledges the night's failure with full honesty. epi de tō rhēmati sou chalasō ta diktya — but upon your word I will let down the nets — the transition from objection to obedience: not upon pragmatic expectation but upon rhēma (your specific word, command). This is the faith-response that occasions the miracle.
6: kai touto poiēsantes synekleisan plēthos ichthyōn poly — doing this, they enclosed a great multitude of fish — synekleisan (enclosed, caught in a net) indicates surrounding the school from below; the verb is also used in Ezekiel 47:10 LXX (fishermen with nets on the shores of the restored land). dierrhēgnuto de ta diktya autōn — and their nets were breaking — the miraculous abundance strains the tools of the trade; the provision exceeds the capacity of the instruments. The net-breaking signals the completeness of the miracle rather than a mundane good catch.
7: kai kateneusan tois metochois en tō heterō ploiō tou elthontas syllabesthai autois — they signalled to their partners in the other boat to come and help them — kataneuō (to signal by nodding or gesturing) implies they cannot shout over the noise or the distance; the partners in the other boat are summoned by gesture. Metochoi (partners, sharers) indicates a fishing cooperative; James and John are identified as partners (koinōnoi, Luke 5:10) of Simon. kai ēlthon kai eplēsan amphotera ta ploia, hōste bythizesthai auta — they came and filled both boats so full they were sinking — bytizō (to sink, submerge) dramatizes the scale; two full boats sinking under fish is the visual of abundance beyond capacity.
8: idōn de Simōn Petros prosepesen tois gonasin Iēsou legōn, Exelthe ap emou, hoti anēr hamartolos eimi, Kyrie — when Simon Peter saw, he fell before Jesus's knees saying: Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, Lord — prosepesen tois gonasin (fell at the knees) is the posture of supplication or prostration before the holy. The response echoes Isaiah's throne-room vision: "Woe to me, for I am undone! I am a man of unclean lips" (Isaiah 6:5) — the encounter with the holy produces consciousness of sin, not just wonder. Hamartolos (sinner) is Luke's most frequent characterization of those who need salvation (Luke 5:30,32; Luke 7:34,37,39; Luke 15:1,2,7,10; Luke 18:13; Luke 19:7); it is a self-designation in the presence of recognized holiness.
9: thambos gar perieschev auton kai pantas tous syn autō — for astonishment had seized him and all those who were with him — thambos (astonishment, awe) is the reaction to the numinous, the presence of power beyond human category. The same word is used in 4:36 after the exorcism; here it follows a miracle of provision. The astonishment is specifically connected to the catch: epi tē agra tōn ichthyōn hōn synelabon — at the catch of fish which they had taken together. The eyewitness testimony of overwhelm is Luke's authentication device throughout the Gospel.
10: homoiōs de kai Iakōbon kai Iōannēn, huious Zebedaiou, hoi ēsan koinōnoi tō Simōni — similarly also James and John, sons of Zebedee, who were partners with Simon — koinōnoi (partners, associates in a business venture) is a commercial term; the apostolic nucleus is a family fishing business. kai eipen pros ton Simōna ho Iēsous, Mē phobou — Jesus said to Simon: Do not be afraid — the comfort-phrase in the presence of the numinous, identical to the angel's words to Zechariah (Luke 1:13), to Mary (Luke 1:30), and to the shepherds (Luke 2:10). apo tou nyn anthrōpous esē zōgrōn — from now on you will be catching people alive — zōgreō (to catch alive, take captive alive — a hunting/fishing term) transforms the vocational metaphor: fish caught die; people "caught" by proclamation gain life.
11: kai katagagontes ta ploia epi tēn gēn, aphentes panta, ēkolouthēsan autō — pulling the boats onto the land, leaving everything, they followed him — the response to the call is total abandonment: aphentes panta (having left all things). Luke uses the same phrase at Levi's call (Luke 5:28) and later generalizes it (18:28 Peter's claim, 18:29 Jesus's promise). The two boats full of fish — a windfall catch worth a season's income — are left on the shore. This is Luke's discipleship definition at its starkest: the net full of fish abandoned with the nets.
12: kai egeneto en tō einai auton en mia tōn poleōn kai idou anēr plērēs lepras — as he was in one of the cities, a man full of leprosy — plērēs lepras (full of leprosy) — Luke's medical term: the disease is at an advanced, total stage. Lepra in the NT encompasses a range of skin conditions classified under Levitical law (Lev 13-14), not only modern Hansen's disease. The man is ritually excluded from community (Leviticus 13:45-46) and approaches Jesus in violation of social protocol. pesōn epi prosōpon edeēthē autou — falling on his face, he begged him — deeomai (to beg, implore) is the prayer-verb; the leper approaches Jesus as a suppliant approaching God.
13: kai ekteinas tēn cheira hēpsato autou legōn, Thelō, katharisthēti — extending his hand, he touched him saying: I am willing; be cleansed — the touch is the scandal: Jesus could have healed by word alone (cf. Luke 4:35,39) but chooses to touch the ritually impure. Under Levitical law, touching a metzora (one with a skin disease) transmitted impurity (Leviticus 5:3); Jesus does not become impure but transfers purity. Thelō (I am willing) answers the leper's implicit doubt ("if you are willing," Luke 5:12); divine will aligns with human need. kai eutheōs hē lepra apēlthen ap autou — and immediately the leprosy departed from him — apēlthen (went away, departed) — the same verb for demon-departure.
14: kai autos parēggeilen autō mēdeni eipein — he commanded him to tell no one — the messianic silence command (cf. Luke 4:41); the proclamation of identity through miracles is premature. alla apelthōn deixon seautō tō hierai — but going, show yourself to the priest — Lev 14 prescribed a detailed seven-day purification ritual for the restored leper: the priest examined the healed person, performed two-bird sacrifice, and pronounced cleanness. The instruction to comply with the Mosaic ritual demonstrates Jesus's affirmation of Torah even as he transcends its mechanisms. kai prospheron peri tou katharismou sou kathōs prosetaxen Mōysēs — and offer the things concerning your cleansing as Moses commanded — the restored leper rejoins the covenant community through its own prescribed means.
15: diērcheto de mallon ho logos peri autou — the word about him spread all the more — the Lukan irony: silence was commanded (Luke 5:14) but news spread further. Ho logos (the word, the report) is Luke's ambiguous term — it will become the technical term for the apostolic gospel proclamation in Acts. kai synērchonto ochloi polloi akouein kai therapeuesthai apo tōn astheneiōn autōn — crowds came together to hear and to be healed from their infirmities — the dual purpose of the crowds is Lukan: they come both to hear (the word) and to be healed (physical restoration). Luke consistently pairs proclamation and healing as twin dimensions of the kingdom's arrival.
16: autos de ēn hypochōrōn en tais erēmois kai proseuchomenos — but he was withdrawing in the deserted places and praying — Luke's characteristic prayer note: this is the third Lukan mention of Jesus withdrawing to pray (cf. 3:21 at baptism; 4:42 after the Capernaum healings), and will continue at every major turning point (Luke 6:12; Luke 9:18,28-29; Luke 11:1; Luke 22:41-44). Hypochōreō (to withdraw, retire) is stronger than Mark's aperchomai; the imperfect ēn ... proseuchomenos (was regularly praying) indicates a pattern, not a single occasion. Luke presents prayer as the foundation of Jesus's ministry, not an interruption to it.
17: kai egeneto en mia tōn hēmerōn kai autos ēn didaskōn — one day as he was teaching — the scene is set: Jesus is teaching in a house. kai ēsan kathēmenoi Pharisaioi kai nomodidaskaloi hoi ēsan elēlythotes ek pasēs kōmēs tēs Galilaias kai Ioudaias kai Ierousalēm — Pharisees and teachers of the law were sitting, who had come from every village of Galilee, Judea, and Jerusalem — Luke stresses the representative gathering of all three regions: Galilee (north), Judea (south), and Jerusalem (the capital). This is a formal investigative delegation, not a chance encounter. kai dynamis Kyriou ēn eis to iasthai auton — and the power of the Lord was present for him to heal — Luke notes the Spirit's presence as the enabling condition for the miracle that follows.
18: kai idou andres pherontes epi klinēs anthrōpon hos ēn paralalymenos — men were bringing a paralysed man on a stretcher — klinē is a bed or stretcher; Luke's version (vs. Mark's krabatton, a pallet) uses slightly more polished Greek vocabulary. Paralalymenos (perfect passive participle of paralyō, to loose at the side — a medical term for limb paralysis) is Luke's medical vocabulary. The four bearers (Mark 2:3 specifies four; Luke does not count them) could not access Jesus because of the crowd; the house is packed.
19: kai mē heurontes poias eisenengkōsin auton dia ton ochlon, anabantes epi to dōma — not finding a way to bring him in through the crowd, going up onto the roof — dōma (flat roof, housetop) is the standard Palestinian roof accessible by exterior stairs. Luke's version describes removing roof tiles (dia tōn keramōn, through the tiles — a Hellenistic construction detail vs. Mark's digging through earth and thatch), reflecting an audience familiar with tiled roofs rather than Palestinian mud construction. kathēkan auton syn tō klinidiō eis to meson emprosthen tou Iēsou — they lowered him with the small stretcher into the middle in front of Jesus — the centering detail: right before Jesus, unavoidable.
20: kai idōn tēn pistin autōn eipen, Anthrōpe, apheontai soi hai hamartiai sou — seeing their faith, he said: Friend, your sins are forgiven — tēn pistin autōn (their faith) encompasses both the paralyzed man and his bearers; faith visible in action (the creative roof-entry) is what Jesus perceives. Anthrōpe (man, friend) is a direct but not demeaning address. The forgiveness precedes the healing — Luke's ordering makes the theological point: forgiveness is the primary need; physical restoration follows as its sign. Apheontai (have been forgiven, perfect passive) declares completed forgiveness in the divine passive.
21: kai ērxanto dialogizesthai hoi grammateis kai hoi Pharisaioi legontes, Tis estin houtos hos lalei blasphēmias — the scribes and Pharisees began to reason: who is this who speaks blasphemy? — dialogizesthai (to reason, deliberate — Luke uses this for internal, often hostile calculation, cf. Luke 1:29; Luke 2:35; Luke 3:15) is internal, not voiced aloud; Jesus perceives it (Luke 5:22). The charge of blasphemy is the formal religious accusation: blasphēmia (defaming, reviling) of God — specifically, usurping divine prerogatives. tis dynatai hamartias aphienai ei mē monos ho theos — who can forgive sins except God alone? — this is the correct theological premise; the scribes' error is not the premise but the failure to recognize who stands before them.
22: epignous de ho Iēsous tous dialogismous autōn apokritheis eipen pros autous — Jesus, knowing their deliberations, answered and said to them — epignous (having known, perceived — aorist of epiginōskō, intensive knowing) indicates supernatural cognition of unexpressed thoughts, not inference from facial expressions. This is one of Luke's consistent demonstrations of Jesus's divine omniscience (cf. Luke 6:8; Luke 9:47; Luke 11:17). ti dialogizesthe en tais kardiais hymōn — why are you deliberating in your hearts? — the internal reasoning is exposed and addressed directly; the question confronts rather than accuses.
23: ti estin eukopōteron, eipein, Apheontai soi hai hamartiai sou, ē eipein, Egeire kai peripatei — which is easier: to say, your sins are forgiven, or to say, rise and walk? — the rhetorical question addresses what is easier to say, not to do. Pronouncing forgiveness is easier to say because it cannot be immediately verified; commanding physical healing is immediately verifiable and the healer is immediately accountable to the result. By performing the visible miracle, Jesus provides public verification of the invisible divine action (forgiveness) that no human can authenticate.
24: hina de eidēte hoti ho huios tou anthrōpou exousian echei epi tēs gēs aphienai hamartias — but so that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins — ho huios tou anthrōpou (the Son of Man) is Jesus's characteristic self-designation in the Synoptics; in Daniel 7:13-14 the Son of Man receives from the Ancient of Days universal authority, glory, and dominion. Jesus applies this Danielic title to the one who has exousian ... epi tēs gēs — authority on earth (not merely eschatological authority, but present, operative authority in the physical world). The healing that follows is the public demonstration of this claim.
25: kai parachrēma anastas enōpion autōn aras eph ho kateiketo apēlthen eis ton oikon autou doxazōn ton theon — immediately he rose before them, took up what he had lain on, and went home glorifying God — parachrēma (immediately, at once — Luke's preferred term for instantaneous miraculous healing, cf. Luke 1:64; Luke 4:39; Luke 8:44,47; Luke 13:13; Luke 18:43; Acts 3:7; Luke 5:10) signals the completeness of divine power. The former paralytic carries his own stretcher — the instrument of his helplessness becomes the trophy of his healing. Doxazōn ton theon (glorifying God) is the proper response: the man attributes the healing to God, not merely to the healer.
26: kai ekstasis elaben hapantas kai edoxazon ton theon — astonishment seized all and they were glorifying God — ekstasis (ecstasy, being outside oneself, displacement by overwhelming experience) is the strongest Greek term for stupefied amazement; the crowd is beyond ordinary response. kai eplēsthēsan phobou legontes hoti eidomen paradoxa sēmeron — they were filled with fear, saying: we have seen remarkable things today — paradoxa (wonderful things, paradoxes — things contrary to ordinary expectation) is a Lukan hapax; the crowd's assessment captures the epistemological shock of divine intrusion into the ordinary. Sēmeron (today) echoes Luke 4:21 — the Jubilee-era of divine favor is the present moment of encounter with Jesus.
27: kai meta tauta exēlthen kai etheasato telōnēn onomati Leuin kathēmenon epi to telōnion — after these things he went out and saw a tax collector named Levi sitting at the tax office — telōnēs (tax collector, toll collector) was a contracted collector for Rome, generally detested as both a collaborator and a notorious extortioner; telōnion (tax booth) is the collection point on a road or at a border. Levi (Matthew in Matthew 9:9) sat at one of the Capernaum customs stations on the Via Maris trade route. kai eipen autō, Akolouthei moi — and he said to him: Follow me — the call is identical in form to Simon's (cf. Luke 5:10); no explanation, no preliminary discussion, one command.
28: kai katalipōn panta anastas ēkolouthei autō — leaving everything, he rose and followed him — Levi's response is more total than Simon's in one respect: the tax collector abandoned a profitable licensed business with no possibility of return (a fishing boat can be reclaimed; a tax contract relinquished is gone). Katalipōn panta (having left everything) is Luke's emphatic phrase for complete discipleship abandonment (cf. Luke 5:11; Luke 18:28-29). The imperfect ēkolouthei (was following, began to follow) signals the ongoing nature of the following that begins here.
29: kai epoiēsen dochēn megalēn Leuis autō en tē oikia autou — and Levi made a great banquet for him in his house — dochē (reception, banquet — a formal feast given in someone's honor) is a Lukan word (also Luke 14:13); it signals a celebration of the call. kai ēn ochlos polys telōnōn kai allōn hoi ēsan met autōn katakeimenoi — there was a large crowd of tax collectors and others who were reclining with them — katakeimai (to recline at table) is the Greek dining posture for a formal meal. The gathering is Levi's professional network: tax collectors and the socially marginal ("others") who shared their stigma.
30: kai egongyzon hoi Pharisaioi kai hoi grammateis autōn pros tous mathētas autou legontes — the Pharisees and their scribes murmured against his disciples saying — egongyzon (were murmuring, grumbling) is the LXX term for Israel's wilderness murmuring against Moses (Exodus 15:24; Luke 16:7-8; Luke 17:3; Numbers 14:2,27; Psalms 106:25); Luke's use of this exact word signals the Pharisees as recapitulating Israel's wilderness sin of complaining against God's appointed leader. Dia ti meta tōn telōnōn kai hamartoōlōn esthiete kai pinete — why do you eat and drink with tax collectors and sinners? — the table-fellowship challenge: eating with the am ha-aretz (people of the land) and tax collectors made a rabbi ritually suspect and socially compromised.
31: kai apokritheis ho Iēsous eipen pros autous, Ou chreian echousin hoi hygiainontes iatrou alla hoi kakōs echontes — Jesus answered them: those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are sick — the physician proverb is known from Greek sources (Plutarch, Diogenes Laertius) and is probably a common wisdom saying. Luke's ironic appropriateness: he is addressing the physician-metaphor to a physician's audience (Luke himself). Hoi hygiainontes (those who are well, healthy) vs. hoi kakōs echontes (those who are doing badly, having it badly = the sick) sets up the social analogy Jesus will make explicit in Luke 5:32.
32: ouk elēlytha kalesai dikaious alla hamartōlous eis metanoian — I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance — kalesai (to call, summon) is the vocabulary of divine election and invitation; the same word is used for God's call of Abraham, Israel, and the prophets. Dikaious (righteous ones) is ironic: the Pharisees consider themselves righteous; Jesus does not contest their self-assessment but reframes the mission. Hamartoōlous eis metanoian — sinners to repentance — metanoia (change of mind, turning) is distinctively Lukan; it occurs more in Luke-Acts than in all other NT writings combined and is the defining characteristic of the kingdom's response requirement.
33: hoi de eipen pros auton, Hoi mathētai Iōannou nēsteuousin pyknōs kai deēseis poioyntai — they said to him: John's disciples fast often and make prayers — nēsteuousin pyknōs (fast frequently, regularly) — the Pharisees (m. Taanit) fasted Mondays and Thursdays in addition to the mandatory Day of Atonement fast; John's ascetic movement fasted similarly. Deēseis poiountai (they make/perform prayers) — formal prayer as religious discipline. homoiōs kai hoi tōn Pharisaiōn, hoi de soi esthiousin kai pinousin — and likewise the Pharisees', but yours eat and drink — the accusation is framed as a contrast between two recognized religious traditions (Johannine, Pharisaic) and Jesus's notably different practice.
34: ho de Iēsous eipen pros autous, Mē dynasthe tous huious tou nymphōnos en hō ho numphios met autōn estin poiēsai nēsteusai — Jesus said to them: can you make the sons of the bridal chamber fast while the bridegroom is with them? — huioi tou nymphōnos (sons of the bridal chamber = wedding guests, groomsmen) is a Semitic idiom for those belonging to the bridal party. The nymphios (bridegroom) is a well-established image for YHWH in relationship to Israel (Isaiah 62:5; Jeremiah 2:2; Hosea 2:16-20; Ezek 16). Jesus applies the bridegroom image to himself without explanation; the audience would understand the implication: the messianic age has arrived, and fasting (mourning) is incompatible with the bridegroom's presence.
35: eleusontai de hēmerai kai hotan aparthē ap autōn ho numphios — but days will come when the bridegroom is taken away from them — aparthē (is taken away, removed by force — aorist passive of apairō) is a passive that implies external force, not natural departure; this is a cryptic Passion reference, the first in Luke. The wedding-feast of the messianic era will be interrupted by the violent removal of the bridegroom — an event only later identified explicitly as the crucifixion. tote nēsteusousin en ekeinais tais hēmerais — then they will fast in those days — fasting in the post-Easter era is legitimate, but as grief for the absent bridegroom, not as religious performance.
36: elegen de kai parabolēn pros autous — he was also telling a parable to them — the two parables of incompatible structures address the deeper question behind the fasting-debate: the new era is qualitatively incompatible with the old order, not merely quantitatively different. Oudeis epilamma schismas apo himatiou kainou epiballei epi himation palaion — no one tears a patch from a new garment and sews it on an old garment — the garment parable: the new garment is destroyed to patch the old, and the patch from the new does not match the old anyway (shrinkage difference). The new era cannot be used as a resource to repair the old religious order; it would ruin both.
37: kai oudeis ballei oinon neon eis askous palaious — and no one puts new wine into old wineskins — askous (wineskins, leather bags made from goatskin) were used for wine storage; old skins had already been stretched to their limit by previous fermentation. New wine (oinos neos) continues to ferment and expand; old skins cannot accommodate the expansion and burst. ei de mē ge, rēxei ho oinos ho neos tous askous — otherwise the new wine will burst the wineskins — the structural incompatibility is not a judgment on the old but a statement of physical necessity. The new era of the Spirit is not compatible with the containing structures of Second Temple Judaism's religious economy.
38: alla oinon neon eis askous kainous blēteon — but new wine must be put into new wineskins — kainous (new in quality, fresh, unused — vs. neos which is new in time) is Luke's word for the eschatologically new. The dei-equivalent blēteon (must be put, verbal adjective of necessity) makes this a structural requirement, not a preference. The new covenant community, the church that Luke will narrate in Acts, is the new wineskin adequate to contain the Spirit's new wine. The parable does not deprecate the old but insists on structural correspondence between content and container.
39: kai oudeis piōn palaion thelei neon, legei gar ho palaios chrēstos estin — and no one after drinking old wine desires new, for they say: the old is good — this final saying is Luke's addition (absent from Matthew and Mark) and is often read as ironic comment on the Pharisees' preference for the old order. Chrēstos (good, pleasant, well-suited, palatable) is the vocabulary of practical usefulness rather than moral excellence. The person habituated to the old wine of Second Temple piety does not feel a deficit; the palatability of the familiar obscures the need for the new. Luke presents this as an explanation of why the religious establishment resists Jesus's new era — not malice but acquired taste.
